1:
On Exodus 4:1 — “And Moses answered and said, But, behold, they will not believe me, nor hearken unto my voice: for they will say, The LORD hath not appeared unto thee” — the witnesses mark Moses’ objection of unbelief.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: Moses meant a positive conviction he would not be listened to; his faith was weak. The Puritan Henry: he accepts the commission with great reluctance, more from cowardice and unbelief than humility. The Reformed Calvin: he obeyed hesitatingly, not from stubbornness but timidity, shrinking from the yoke. Wesley: he feared they would reject him as before, unless he showed a sign.

The verse opens Moses’ objection. He fears Israel will not believe that God appeared to him. Weak faith breeds the dread of rejection.

So the witnesses see Moses, called to so great a work, raise his fear that Israel will not believe him. The witnesses note the objection: “they will not believe me, nor hearken unto my voice” — Moses anticipates that his countrymen will reject his message, saying “the LORD hath not appeared unto thee.” They mark Ellicott’s point that the word rendered “behold” expresses not a mere “perhaps” but a positive conviction; Moses was sure he would not be heeded, a sign his faith was weak. They observe the differing judgments of the expositors on this reluctance. Henry, while granting it may partly spring from humility, owns that here was too much cowardice, slothfulness, and unbelief; Calvin reads it more gently, that Moses obeyed hesitatingly not from a stubborn refusal to bear the yoke, as unruly beasts do, but from timidity, shrinking under a sense of the task’s greatness. They note Wesley’s observation that Moses remembered how Israel had once rejected him (Exodus 2:14) and feared it would be so again unless he could show them a sign. They observe that the objection, though it betrays weakness, is met by God not with rebuke but with gracious provision of signs. They note that Moses’ fear of man’s unbelief is the first of a string of hesitations. The verse thus opens his objection: Israel will not believe me — which God answers, in the next verses, by the sign of the rod.
2:
On Exodus 4:2 — “And the LORD said unto him, What is that in thine hand? And he said, A rod” — the witnesses mark the question and the shepherd’s rod.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify it. Keil and Delitzsch: the turning of Moses’ staff into a serpent had reference to his calling. Adam Clarke: a staff (matteh), probably his shepherd’s crook, afterwards called the rod of God (Exodus 4:20). Ellicott: most take it for his shepherd’s crook, an ordinary staff. The Reformed Gill: the Lord did not reprove him but gave the hint of a sign.

The verse begins the first sign. God asks what is in Moses’ hand; it is a rod. The shepherd’s staff becomes the instrument of power.

So the witnesses see God answer Moses’ fear not with reproof but with a sign, beginning with the homely object in his hand. The witnesses note the question: “What is that in thine hand?” — God draws Moses’ attention to the simplest of things, the staff he carried. They mark the answer: “A rod” — identified by the expositors as his shepherd’s crook, the ordinary staff of a herdsman; Gill observes that God did not rebuke Moses for asking a sign but rather granted the very means to win Israel’s belief. They observe the significance the witnesses draw from the choice of so mean an instrument: God will work his wonders not with sword or sceptre but with a shepherd’s rod, that the power may plainly be his and not the man’s; the crook with which Moses had kept Jethro’s flock becomes the rod of God. They note Keil and Delitzsch’s point that the sign about to be wrought with this staff had reference to Moses’ calling: the shepherd of sheep is to become the shepherd of God’s people. They observe that the rod, once changed and proved, will be carried through all the wonders to come and named “the rod of God” (Exodus 4:20). They note that God begins with what Moses already holds. The verse thus opens the first sign: the rod in Moses’ hand — which, at God’s word in the next verse, becomes a serpent.
3:
On Exodus 4:3 — “And he said, Cast it on the ground. And he cast it on the ground, and it became a serpent; and Moses fled from before it” — the witnesses mark the rod turned to a serpent.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the creature. Keil and Delitzsch trace the sign. Adam Clarke: a serpent (nachash, a general name, Genesis 3:1), of a kind he had not seen or knew dangerous, for he fled. Ellicott: nachash a generic term for a snake, telling nothing of the species.

The verse works the first sign. The cast-down rod becomes a serpent, and Moses flees. The dead staff turns to living terror.

So the witnesses see the first sign performed: the rod cast down becomes a living serpent. The witnesses note the command and its instant effect: “cast it on the ground… and it became a serpent” — the lifeless shepherd’s staff, at God’s word, is transformed into a living snake. They mark Moses’ reaction: “Moses fled from before it” — he started back in fear, which the expositors take as evidence that this was a real and dangerous serpent, not a mere appearance; the word nachash, Clarke and Ellicott note, is a general term for a snake, telling nothing of the species, but Moses’ flight shows it was no harmless thing. They observe that the suddenness and reality of the change, frightening even to Moses who wrought it, demonstrate the power of God over the very nature of things; what was dead wood becomes a living, fearful creature at his bidding. They note that the sign is fitted both to convince and to awe: a power that can so transform an object is plainly divine. They observe that Moses, though the instrument, is himself startled, showing that the wonder is God’s and not his own contrivance. They note that the serpent will, at the next command, become a rod again in his hand. The verse thus works the first sign: the rod become a serpent, before which Moses flees — which God bids him, in the next verse, take by the tail.
4:
On Exodus 4:4 — “And the LORD said unto Moses, Put forth thine hand, and take it by the tail. And he put forth his hand, and caught it, and it became a rod in his hand” — the witnesses mark the serpent restored to a rod, and Moses’ faith.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the test. Adam Clarke: considering how Moses viewed the serpent, it required considerable faith to obey; he obeyed, and it became the rod again. Ellicott: those who handle poisonous snakes take them by the neck, not the tail; to test Moses’ faith and courage he was bidden the more dangerous way. The Reformed Gill: that he might be assured it was really a serpent.

The verse completes the first sign. Moses, obeying, takes the serpent by the tail and it becomes a rod. Faith grasps what fear would flee.

So the witnesses see the first sign completed, and Moses’ faith tested in the completing. The witnesses note the command, which the expositors mark as a trial of courage: “take it by the tail” — not, as Ellicott observes, by the neck, the way men safely handle dangerous snakes to keep them from biting, but by the tail, the more perilous hold; God bids Moses do the very thing that would seem most to expose him to the serpent’s strike. They mark the faith this required: Clarke notes that, considering how Moses had just fled the creature in fear, it took considerable faith to put forth his hand and seize it as commanded; yet he obeyed. They observe the result: “it became a rod in his hand” — the living serpent, grasped, was instantly the lifeless staff again. They note Gill’s point that this was partly to assure Moses it had been a true serpent and no illusion; the change both ways proved the reality and the power. They observe that the sign, thus completed, demonstrates God’s mastery over creation in both directions — rod to serpent, serpent to rod — and trains Moses in the obedience of faith that overcomes fear. They note that the instrument is now ready, proved in his hand. The verse thus completes the first sign: the serpent caught becomes a rod, Moses’ faith obeying — a sign meant, the next verse says, that Israel may believe.
5:
On Exodus 4:5 — “That they may believe that the LORD God of their fathers… hath appeared unto thee” — the witnesses mark the purpose of the sign.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the elliptical sentence. Adam Clarke and Wesley: an unfinished speech, to be completed — “do this before them, that they may believe.” Ellicott: God’s words continuing, the action of Moses parenthetic. The Reformed Calvin: the power was given him to repeat the miracle, to gain credit with Israel and repress Pharaoh.

The verse states the aim. The sign is given that Israel may believe God appeared to Moses. The wonder is for the people’s faith.

So the witnesses see God declare the purpose of the sign: the faith of Israel. The witnesses note the elliptical form the expositors observe: the sentence is unfinished, to be supplied as “Do this before them, that they may believe that the LORD God of their fathers… hath appeared unto thee”; Ellicott reads the description of Moses’ action (v. 4) as a parenthesis, God’s words running on into this verse. They mark the object of the believing: that the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the covenant God of their fathers, had truly appeared to Moses and sent him; the sign is to authenticate his divine commission. They observe Calvin’s point that the power of working the wonder was not given to Moses once only but to be repeated as needed, both to win the credit of the Israelites and to repress the audacity of Pharaoh; the sign is a standing credential. They note that the miracle was not for Moses’ own conviction — his faith had been confirmed at the burning bush — but for the people’s; God meets their anticipated unbelief with visible proof. They observe that God graciously condescends to the weakness of faith, granting signs that the doubting may be persuaded. They note that the whole aim is belief in the divine appearing and mission. The verse thus states the purpose: that Israel may believe the LORD appeared to Moses — to which God adds, in the next verses, a second and third sign.
6:
On Exodus 4:6 — “And the LORD said furthermore… Put now thine hand into thy bosom. And he… took it out, behold, his hand was leprous as snow” — the witnesses mark the second sign of the leprous hand.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the disease. Adam Clarke: the leprosy in thin white scales, whence the Greek lepra. Ellicott: the worst form, the “white disease,” the skin glossy white, every hair white as wool. The Reformed Calvin: Moses was instructed that what is in greatest vigour withers at God’s command, and the dry is restored. Wesley: it signified the diseases Moses would bring on Egypt and remove.

The verse gives the second sign. Moses’ hand, put in his bosom, comes out leprous. Health turns to corruption at God’s word.

So the witnesses see God add a second sign in Moses’ own body: the sudden leprosy. The witnesses note the act: “put now thine hand into thy bosom… behold, his hand was leprous as snow” — at God’s word the hand drawn from his breast was struck with leprosy, white as snow. They mark the expositors’ description of the disease: Clarke notes the thin white scales from which the Greek name comes; Ellicott identifies it as the worst form, the “white disease,” in which the skin turns glossy white and the hair white as wool — the very marks the law later gives for the leper. They observe the lesson Calvin draws: by this sign Moses was taught that what is in fullest vigour and health withers in a moment at God’s command, and what is dead and corrupt is at his word restored; God kills and makes alive. They note the significance Wesley finds: the leprous and healed hand signified that Moses, by God’s power, should bring sore diseases and plagues upon Egypt and at his prayer remove them; and that Israel, leprous and defiled in their bondage, should be cleansed. They observe that this sign, wrought in Moses’ own flesh, is more startling and personal than the first; God shows his power over the body as over the rod. They note that the sign awaits its undoing in the next verse. The verse thus gives the second sign: the hand made leprous — which God, in the next verse, restores.
7:
On Exodus 4:7 — “And he said, Put thine hand into thy bosom again… and, behold, it was turned again as his other flesh” — the witnesses mark the hand restored.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the sign: the hand put again into the bosom is plucked out whole as the rest of his flesh. The Reformed Gill: the leprosy on it healed and made sound at God’s command.

The verse completes the second sign. Moses’ leprous hand, put back, is made whole. God heals as readily as he smites.

So the witnesses see the second sign completed in the instant healing of the leprous hand. The witnesses note the command and effect: “put thine hand into thy bosom again… and, behold, it was turned again as his other flesh” — the hand that had come out white with leprosy, put back into the breast, is drawn out wholly healed, sound as the rest of his flesh. They mark the completeness of the cure: no scar, no remnant of the disease; the restoration is as sudden and perfect as the smiting. They observe Gill’s plain sense that the leprosy was healed and the hand made sound at God’s word. They note the double lesson the witnesses drew from the first half of the sign now confirmed: as God in a moment can strike the healthiest flesh with the most loathsome and (humanly) incurable disease, so in a moment he can cleanse and heal it; both the wounding and the healing are in his hand. They observe that leprosy was reckoned beyond the cure of men, so that to heal it instantly was the clearer proof of divine power; the same hand that signifies the plagues God will bring on Egypt signifies also the mercy by which he will remove them. They note that the two motions of the sign — corruption and cure — together display God’s absolute power over life and health. They observe that Moses is given a second credential. The verse thus completes the sign: the hand restored whole — with a third sign, the water to blood, added in the next verses.
8:
On Exodus 4:8 — “If they will not believe thee, neither hearken to the voice of the first sign, that they will believe the voice of the latter sign” — the witnesses mark the voice of the signs.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Adam Clarke: some would yield to the first miracle, others hesitate till the second. Ellicott: “the voice of the sign” — the voice the sign itself utters (cf. Psalm 105:27). Wesley: God’s works have a voice to speak to us, to be diligently observed. The Reformed Calvin: God took away every handle for doubt, heaping up the measure.

The verse provides for partial unbelief. If the first sign fails to persuade, the second may. The signs have a voice to convince.

So the witnesses see God provide against the possibility that one sign alone might not convince. The witnesses note the gracious anticipation: “if they will not believe… the first sign… they will believe the voice of the latter” — God foresees that some may yield to the first miracle and others hold out until the second, and so furnishes more than one proof. They mark the striking phrase “the voice of the sign,” which the expositors explain: as Ellicott puts it, not Moses’ voice attested by the sign, but the voice that the sign itself, as it were, utters — the wonder speaks, declaring God’s message; Wesley draws the lesson that God’s works have a voice to speak to us, which we must diligently observe. They observe Clarke’s point that the two signs meet differing degrees of stubbornness: the more readily persuaded will believe at the first, the more doubtful only at the second. They note Calvin’s observation that God thereby took away from the people every handle for doubt, heaping up the measure of proof to overflowing, so that unbelief might be without excuse. They observe the condescension of God to human weakness: he multiplies signs rather than leave any sincere doubter unpersuaded. They note that the very signs preach. The verse thus provides for partial unbelief: the voice of the latter sign for those unmoved by the first — and a third, the next verse adds, against the wholly obstinate.
9:
On Exodus 4:9 — “And if they will not believe also these two signs… thou shalt take of the water of the river… and the water… shall become blood upon the dry land” — the witnesses mark the third sign of water to blood.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: the verb repeated in the Hebrew intensifies it — “shall assuredly become blood”; the signs chosen for facility of exhibition, yet significant. The Reformed Gill: these were done again in Egypt, yet some might still disbelieve; a third given. Calvin: God adds a third sign, taking away all excuse.

The verse gives the third sign. Nile water poured out shall become blood on dry land. The river of Egypt is made a token of judgment.

So the witnesses see God provide a third and weightiest sign for the most obstinate unbelief. The witnesses note the gracious provision: “if they will not believe also these two signs” — should even the rod and the leprous hand fail to persuade, a third proof is given. They mark the sign itself: “thou shalt take of the water of the river… and the water… shall become blood upon the dry land” — water drawn from the Nile and poured out upon the ground would turn to blood. They observe Ellicott’s note that the Hebrew repeats the verb for emphasis, “shall assuredly become blood,” a sure and certain transformation. They note the expositors’ observation that the signs were chosen in part for the ease with which Moses could perform them before the people, yet each carried meaning: this third sign, turning the Nile’s water to blood, pointed forward to the first of the great plagues by which Egypt would be smitten, and struck at the very river the Egyptians revered. They observe Calvin’s and Gill’s point that God thus heaped up his proofs, leaving the obstinate without excuse; three signs in ascending force meet three degrees of doubt. They note that the water of life turned to blood is an ominous token, fitting the judgment to come on Egypt. They observe that God furnishes Moses fully for his mission. The verse thus gives the third sign: Nile water become blood on dry land — after which Moses, in the next verse, raises a new objection.
10:
On Exodus 4:10 — “And Moses said… O my Lord, I am not eloquent… but I am slow of speech, and of a slow tongue” — the witnesses mark Moses’ objection of weak speech.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the phrase. Keil and Delitzsch and Clarke: “not a man of words”; words did not come readily, and he had difficulty uttering them. The Puritan Henry: now it can no longer be imputed to humility, but to cowardice, slothfulness, and unbelief. The Reformed Calvin: Moses catches at every word of escape, trembling at the task’s importance. Wesley: a great philosopher and statesman, yet no orator.

The verse gives a new objection. Moses pleads that he is slow of speech, no man of words. He shrinks behind his lack of eloquence.

So the witnesses see Moses, the signs given, raise yet another objection — his want of eloquence. The witnesses note the plea: “I am not eloquent… but I am slow of speech, and of a slow tongue” — literally, as Clarke and Keil note, “not a man of words”; Moses claims that words do not come readily to him and that he stammers or speaks with difficulty, both formerly and now that God has spoken to him. They mark the harsher judgment the expositors begin to pass on this reluctance. Henry observes that this objection can no longer be excused as humility, but betrays cowardice, slothfulness, and unbelief; Calvin too notes that Moses now catches at every word of escape, seeking to force himself from the task, his distrust of his own powers making him hesitating and timid. They observe Wesley’s balanced portrait: Moses was a great philosopher, statesman, and divine, of clear head and solid judgment, yet not a fluent or ready speaker, and so thought himself unfit to plead before Pharaoh. They note that the objection, whatever truth it held, set Moses’ own weakness against God’s call, as if his lack disqualified what God commanded. They observe that this is the third hesitation, and the one that will draw God’s anger. They note that Moses looks at himself rather than at the God who sends him. The verse thus raises the objection of weak speech — which God answers, in the next verse, by asking who made man’s mouth.
11:
On Exodus 4:11 — “And the LORD said unto him, Who hath made man’s mouth? or who maketh the dumb, or deaf… have not I the LORD?” — the witnesses mark God’s answer from his creative power.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: Jehovah both could and would provide for this defect, having made man’s mouth and made dumb or deaf. Adam Clarke: he who formed the organs of speech can give utterance; do not provoke him. The Reformed Calvin: here is the cause why Moses’ hesitation was reprehensible — he did not look up to God, who needs no human aid.

The verse answers the objection. God who made the mouth, the dumb and deaf, can surely fit Moses to speak. The Creator overrules the creature’s excuse.

So the witnesses see God meet Moses’ plea of weak speech with an appeal to his own creative sovereignty. The witnesses note the searching questions: “Who hath made man’s mouth? or who maketh the dumb, or deaf, or the seeing, or the blind?” — God points Moses to the truth that the mouth itself, with its faculty of speech, is God’s own making, and that he it is who appoints to one man a ready tongue and to another dumbness, to one sight and to another blindness; all the powers and defects of human faculties are from him. They mark the conclusion he draws: “have not I the LORD?” — the God who made the mouth can surely supply its want and fit even a slow tongue to speak his message. They observe Clarke’s pointed application: he who formed the organs of speech can give utterance, and can also take away the gift; therefore Moses must not provoke him by refusing. They note Calvin’s discernment of why Moses’ hesitation was blameworthy: arrested by a sense of his own infirmity, he failed to look up to God, who is above the want of any human aid and easily accomplishes whatever he decrees. They observe that the answer rebukes the root of all such objections — the looking to oneself rather than to the all-sufficient God. They note that God claims power over the very faculty Moses pleaded against. The verse thus answers the objection: the Maker of the mouth can fit Moses to speak — and bids him, in the next verse, go, with the promise of help.
12:
On Exodus 4:12 — “Now therefore go, and I will be with thy mouth, and teach thee what thou shalt say” — the witnesses mark the promise of divine help in speaking.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the promise. Keil and Delitzsch trace it. Adam Clarke: the Targums read “my Word shall be with thy mouth.” Ellicott: to suggest words and assist utterance (cf. Matthew 10:19); compare the reluctance of Jeremiah (Jeremiah 1:6) and God’s dealings with him. The Reformed Gill: God would put words into his mouth and make it speak readily.

The verse gives the command and promise. Go, and God will be with his mouth and teach him. Divine help meets the plea of weakness.

So the witnesses see God turn his answer into a command joined with a gracious promise. The witnesses note the imperative: “Now therefore go” — the objection answered, Moses is simply bidden to obey and set out on his mission. They mark the twofold promise that meets his fear: “I will be with thy mouth, and teach thee what thou shalt say” — God undertakes both to assist Moses’ utterance and to supply the very words; as Ellicott notes, to suggest the matter and aid the speaking. They observe the parallel the expositors draw with the New Testament promise to the disciples, that in the hour of need it would be given them what to speak, the Spirit speaking in them (Matthew 10:19); and with God’s answer to the reluctant Jeremiah, “I have put my words in thy mouth” (Jeremiah 1:6). They note Gill’s point that God would put words into Moses’ mouth and make it speak readily and powerfully, so that Moses, far from being unfit, became (as later Scripture says) mighty in word as in deed. They observe that the promise removes all ground for the excuse: the slow tongue is no hindrance when God himself is with the mouth. They note that the sufficiency lies not in Moses but in God who sends and equips him. The verse thus gives the command and promise: go, and I will be with thy mouth — which, sadly, Moses still resists in the next verse.
13:
On Exodus 4:13 — “And he said, O my Lord, send, I pray thee, by the hand of him whom thou wilt send” — the witnesses mark Moses’ final reluctance, and a debated meaning.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: a curt, impatient, scarcely reverent speech — send by whom thou wilt; Moses means God would do better to send another. Adam Clarke notes that many ancient and modern commentators thought Moses prayed here for the mission of the Messiah. The Reformed Calvin records the patristic reading and weighs it.

The verse gives Moses’ last objection. He begs God to send by another’s hand. His meaning is disputed.

So the witnesses see Moses, all his objections answered, make one last reluctant plea. The witnesses note the words: “send, I pray thee, by the hand of him whom thou wilt send” — a request that someone else be sent in his place. They mark the debate the expositors record over what Moses meant. Some ancient and medieval writers, as Clarke and Calvin note (citing such fathers as Justin, Tertullian, Cyprian, and Eusebius), took the words as a prayer that God would send the promised Messiah, the great Deliverer, rather than Moses — reading a pious longing into the verse. But the witnesses observe that the weight of judgment, ancient and modern, runs against this reading. Ellicott calls it a curt, impatient, and scarcely reverent speech, by which Moses simply means that God would do far better to send some other person; and the very next verse, where God’s anger is kindled, confirms that this was no holy prayer but a faithless shrinking from the task. They note that the kindling of the Lord’s anger shows Moses still seeking to evade his call, having run out of particular objections and now begging in general to be excused. They observe that the request marks the limit of God’s patience with his hesitation. They note that even the meek and faithful may, pressed by fear, resist a clear call. The verse thus gives Moses’ last reluctance: send by another — at which, the next verse says, the anger of the Lord was kindled.
14:
On Exodus 4:14 — “And the anger of the LORD was kindled against Moses… Is not Aaron the Levite thy brother? I know that he can speak well… he cometh forth to meet thee” — the witnesses mark God’s anger and the appointing of Aaron.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace it: Aaron can speak well and comes to meet Moses, glad in heart. Adam Clarke: had Moses prayed for the Messiah, this anger would not have followed; his reluctance is reproved. The Puritan Henry and Wesley: even self-diffidence, when it hinders or clogs duty, is displeasing to God. The Reformed Calvin: there is no better sacrifice than to obey God’s voice (1 Samuel 15:22).

The verse records God’s anger. Provoked at last, God appoints Aaron to speak for Moses. Mercy answers even his faithless reluctance.

So the witnesses see God, at last provoked by Moses’ persistent shrinking, kindle in anger — yet temper the anger with mercy. The witnesses note the anger: “the anger of the LORD was kindled against Moses” — the only such expression toward Moses in the call; his repeated objections, having run from modesty into unbelief, displease God. They mark the lesson the expositors draw: Henry and Wesley observe that even self-diffidence, when it grows extreme and hinders or clogs us in duty, is offensive to God; Calvin recalls that there is no better sacrifice than to obey God’s voice (1 Samuel 15:22), so that the specious excuses of Moses grievously offended. They observe Clarke’s point that this kindling of anger confirms Moses’ words were no pious prayer for the Messiah but a faithless reluctance worthy of reproof. They note, however, the mercy mingled with the displeasure: God provides Aaron — “Is not Aaron the Levite thy brother? I know that he can speak well” — appointing the eloquent brother to be Moses’ spokesman. They observe the tender detail: “he cometh forth to meet thee: and when he seeth thee, he will be glad in his heart” — God has already moved Aaron toward Moses, and assures him of a glad and willing reception. They note that even in anger God does not withdraw the commission but supplies the want. They observe that mercy answers Moses’ weakness. The verse thus records the anger and the provision: Aaron appointed to speak — whose office God defines in the next verses.
15:
On Exodus 4:15 — “And thou shalt speak unto him, and put words in his mouth: and I will be with thy mouth, and with his mouth, and will teach you what ye shall do” — the witnesses mark the joint commission of Moses and Aaron.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the arrangement. Keil and Delitzsch trace it. Adam Clarke: both shall be under the continual inspiration of the Most High. Ellicott: Moses to furnish the matter, Aaron to clothe it in language; God with both mouths. The Reformed Gill: Moses to put the things into Aaron’s mouth, who, being eloquent, would express them fluently. Wesley: even Aaron could not speak to purpose unless God were with his mouth.

The verse defines the partnership. Moses gives Aaron the words; God will be with both mouths. The two together serve under God’s help.

So the witnesses see God set out the working partnership of the two brothers. The witnesses note the chain of communication: “thou shalt speak unto him, and put words in his mouth” — Moses is to receive God’s message and convey it to Aaron, furnishing the matter and substance of what is to be said; Aaron, being eloquent, will then clothe it in fitting language and speak it out fluently. They mark the promise extended to both: “I will be with thy mouth, and with his mouth” — God undertakes to assist both the giving and the speaking; as Ellicott explains, suggesting the matter to Moses and the language to Aaron. They observe Wesley’s pointed note that even Aaron, gifted speaker though he was, could not speak to purpose unless God were with his mouth; without the constant aids of divine grace, the best natural gifts fail. They note the further word, “and will teach you what ye shall do” — God will direct not only their speech but their action, guiding the whole conduct of their mission. They observe the order the arrangement establishes: Moses the source, Aaron the voice, God the power behind both. They note that the joint commission both answers Moses’ objection and binds the brothers together in the work. They observe that all sufficiency, for the one who supplies the words and the one who speaks them, is of God. The verse thus defines the partnership: Moses to put words in Aaron’s mouth, God with both — the relation the next verse states more fully.
16:
On Exodus 4:16 — “And he shall be thy spokesman unto the people: and he shall be, even he shall be to thee instead of a mouth, and thou shalt be to him instead of God” — the witnesses mark the relation of Moses and Aaron.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: Aaron shall speak for thee, be to thee instead of a mouth; thou shalt deliver to him what God communicates. Ellicott: God did not speak to Aaron directly but through Moses; Aaron was to recognize in Moses God’s authority. The Reformed Calvin: God yields to his servant’s prayer, yet confers honour upon Moses, not putting Aaron in his place.

The verse states the order. Aaron is mouth to Moses; Moses is as God to Aaron. The prophet and his spokesman are ranked.

So the witnesses see God define the exact relation between the brothers, a relation that exalts Moses even while granting his request. The witnesses note Aaron’s office toward the people: “he shall be thy spokesman unto the people” — Aaron will declare to Israel and to Pharaoh the message Moses receives, speaking for him. They mark the figure that defines it: “he shall be to thee instead of a mouth” — Aaron serves as Moses’ mouth, voicing what Moses gives him, as a man’s lips utter the thoughts of his mind. They observe the higher term: “and thou shalt be to him instead of God” — Moses stands to Aaron in the place of God, in that God speaks to Moses and Moses to Aaron; as Ellicott notes, God did not address Aaron directly but through Moses, so that Aaron was to recognize in Moses the authority and revelation of God. They note that this language, far from degrading Moses by giving him a spokesman, dignifies him: he is made, as it were, a god to Aaron, the source of the divine word; Calvin observes that God, while yielding to Moses’ reluctance by supplying Aaron, yet confers honour upon Moses rather than displacing him. They observe that the relation foreshadows the office of a prophet, who receives from God and delivers to the people through a spoken word. They note that Moses retains the primacy of revelation. The verse thus ranks the two: Aaron the mouth, Moses as God — the arrangement closed by the command to take the rod in the next verse.
17:
On Exodus 4:17 — “And thou shalt take this rod in thine hand, wherewith thou shalt do signs” — the witnesses mark the rod of authority.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: “this rod” — the rod that had been changed into a serpent; “signs,” those he would perform. The Reformed Calvin: God chose this shepherd’s rod to confound the pride of Pharaoh. Wesley: the staff he carried as a shepherd, that he might not be ashamed of his mean condition; it was to be his staff of authority, instead of sword and sceptre.

The verse closes the commission. Moses is to take the rod for working signs. The shepherd’s staff becomes the badge of God’s power.

So the witnesses see God close the commission by putting the rod, the instrument of the signs, into Moses’ hand. The witnesses note the charge: “thou shalt take this rod in thine hand, wherewith thou shalt do signs” — the same staff that had been turned into a serpent (v. 3), now to be the instrument through which Moses will work the wonders before Pharaoh and Israel. They mark the significance the expositors find in God’s choice of so humble a thing. Calvin observes that God chose this shepherd’s crook on purpose, the more to confound the pride of Pharaoh, that the mighty works might be wrought by a despised instrument and the glory be plainly God’s. They note Wesley’s point that it was the very staff Moses had carried as a shepherd, that he need not be ashamed of the mean condition out of which God called him; and that this rod was now to serve him as his staff of authority, standing in the place of both sword and sceptre — the badge not of his own might but of God’s power working through him. They observe that the rod, once a herdsman’s tool, becomes “the rod of God” (Exodus 4:20), carried through all the plagues and the wonders of the Exodus. They note that the lowliness of the instrument magnifies the worker. They observe that the commission is now complete: the man, the helper, and the rod all provided. The verse thus closes the call: take the rod for the signs — after which Moses, in the next verse, returns to Jethro to take his leave.
18:
On Exodus 4:18 — “And Moses went and returned to Jethro his father in law, and said… Let me go, I pray thee, and return unto my brethren which are in Egypt… And Jethro said… Go in peace” — the witnesses mark Moses taking leave of Jethro.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: when Moses married Zipporah he was probably adopted into the tribe; the close tie made asking leave fitting. The Puritan Henry: Moses would not leave his service without asking, courteous to his benefactor. The Reformed Calvin: surprising that Moses suppressed the vision, speaking only of human feeling — the desire to revisit his brethren.

The verse records his leave-taking. Moses asks Jethro’s permission to return to Egypt, and is sent in peace. He departs with due courtesy and reserve.

So the witnesses see Moses, before setting out, return to Jethro to ask his leave with proper respect. The witnesses note the courtesy: “Let me go, I pray thee, and return unto my brethren which are in Egypt” — Moses, who had been kindly received and long served Jethro as a shepherd, would not depart with his family without asking permission; Henry marks this as the act of one civil to his benefactor and just to his service. They observe the close tie the expositors note: by his marriage to Zipporah, Moses had been received into Jethro’s tribe, so that the asking of leave, even for a journey, was fitting and natural. They mark a reserve in his words that Calvin finds surprising: Moses speaks only of human feeling — the desire to see his brethren and kindred again — and does not mention the divine vision and commission that were the true reason of his going. They note the prudence the witnesses discern in this silence: Moses does not parade the great revelation, but states a modest and sufficient reason, leaving the rest unspoken; perhaps to avoid difficulty, or out of humility about so high a calling. They observe Jethro’s ready and gracious consent: “Go in peace” — a kind dismissal, wishing him well. They note that Moses begins his great errand with quiet propriety, not fanfare. The verse thus records his leave-taking: sent in peace by Jethro — before God, in the next verse, confirms the call to return.
19:
On Exodus 4:19 — “And the LORD said unto Moses in Midian, Go, return into Egypt: for all the men are dead which sought thy life” — the witnesses mark the renewed command and the removal of danger.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: on leaving Midian Moses received another communication. Adam Clarke: a new revelation, after he returned to his father-in-law. Ellicott: Moses had delayed after getting Jethro’s leave, hence the peremptory “Go, return”; the kindred of the murdered Egyptian, not only Pharaoh, had sought his life. The Reformed Calvin and Wesley note this as a second vision urging present obedience.

The verse renews the charge. God bids Moses return, for his enemies are dead. The way is now clear of personal danger.

So the witnesses see God, as Moses prepares to depart, renew the command and remove a lingering fear. The witnesses note the renewed charge: “Go, return into Egypt” — a second word from God, urging Moses to the present execution of the commission already given; Ellicott reads the peremptory tone as a spur, Moses having perhaps delayed even after obtaining Jethro’s leave. They mark the encouragement attached: “for all the men are dead which sought thy life” — the danger that had driven Moses from Egypt forty years before, and that might still have made him fear to return, was now past; those who had sought to kill him for slaying the Egyptian were dead. They observe Ellicott’s note that this included not only the Pharaoh of Exodus 2:23 but the kindred of the murdered man, who might have pursued a blood-feud; all such were gone. They note the tenderness of God in this: knowing the human fear that might still hold Moses back, God removes it by assuring him his enemies are no more; the path is safe. They observe that this second vision both renews the call and clears the way, leaving Moses no ground for further delay on the score of personal peril. They note that God meets not only Moses’ objections but his unspoken fears. They observe that Providence had cleared the obstacles before sending him. The verse thus renews the charge: return, for thy enemies are dead — and Moses, in the next verse, sets out with his family.
20:
On Exodus 4:20 — “And Moses took his wife and his sons, and set them upon an ass, and he returned to the land of Egypt: and Moses took the rod of God in his hand” — the witnesses mark the departure with family and rod.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: his sons — Gershom and another lately born (Exodus 2:22); set on the ass. The Reformed Gill and Calvin: by taking wife and sons Moses showed he meant to dwell in Egypt and believed in the deliverance; the single ass a plain proof how humble his condition. Wesley: the rod of God, his shepherd’s crook, so called as God’s instrument.

The verse records the setting out. Moses takes his family and the rod of God and returns to Egypt. He goes in faith and lowliness.

So the witnesses see Moses obey at last, setting out for Egypt with his family and the divine rod. The witnesses note the company: “Moses took his wife and his sons, and set them upon an ass” — Zipporah and his two sons, Gershom and the lately-born Eliezer; the taking of his whole household showing, as Gill and Calvin observe, that Moses now meant to dwell in Egypt and firmly believed God would work the promised deliverance. They mark the lowliness of the going: the single ass that bore his family is a plain proof, Calvin notes, how humble was Moses’ condition and how slender his substance; the man called to confront the king of Egypt sets out in poverty. They observe the great instrument he carries: “Moses took the rod of God in his hand” — the shepherd’s staff now named “the rod of God,” because it was to be the instrument of his mighty works; Wesley notes the title marks it as God’s own tool of power. They note the contrast the verse holds: a poor man on foot beside an ass-borne family, yet bearing in his hand the rod by which Egypt will be smitten and Israel freed; the weakness of the man and the power of the rod set side by side. They observe that Moses goes forth in faith, committed to the work. They note that the journey to deliver a nation begins in humble obedience. The verse thus records the departure: family and rod, toward Egypt — God adding, in the next verses, instructions for the meeting with Pharaoh.
21:
On Exodus 4:21 — “When thou goest to return into Egypt, see that thou do all those wonders before Pharaoh… but I will harden his heart, that he shall not let the people go” — the witnesses mark the wonders and the hardening of Pharaoh.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: not the three signs but the “portents” before Pharaoh, already put in Moses’ hand. On the hardening: Adam Clarke urges looking at the whole account apart from creeds. Wesley: God hardens after Pharaoh hardened himself, at last permitting Satan to harden it effectually. The Reformed Calvin reads it of God’s decree.

The verse forewarns of resistance. Moses is to work the wonders, but God will harden Pharaoh’s heart. The outcome is disclosed beforehand.

So the witnesses see God forewarn Moses both of the wonders he must work and of the resistance he will meet. The witnesses note the charge: “see that thou do all those wonders before Pharaoh… which I have put in thine hand” — the “wonders” here, the expositors note, are not chiefly the three signs given to convince Israel, but the great portents and plagues to be wrought before Pharaoh, already appointed in God’s counsel and committed to Moses. They mark the sobering forewarning: “but I will harden his heart, that he shall not let the people go” — God tells Moses in advance that the wonders, far from at once moving the king, will be met by a hardened, obstinate refusal. They observe the famous and much-debated question this raises, which the expositors weigh: in what sense God “hardens” Pharaoh’s heart. Several of the witnesses are careful to guard the divine justice. Wesley holds that God hardens Pharaoh only after Pharaoh has repeatedly hardened himself, wilfully shutting his eyes against the light, God at last judicially permitting Satan to harden it effectually. Clarke urges the reader to view the whole account apart from the lens of any creed, finding the difficulty less than supposed when Pharaoh’s own obstinacy is reckoned with. Calvin, by contrast, reads the hardening as the execution of God’s sovereign decree, by which he ordains to display his power in Pharaoh’s resistance. They note that the witnesses divide between the judicial-permissive and the decretal readings, yet agree that Pharaoh’s guilt is his own. They observe that God discloses the hard outcome beforehand, that Moses might not be discouraged when the king resists. The verse thus forewarns: the wonders done, yet Pharaoh hardened — the ground on which God sends the message of his firstborn son in the next verses.
22:
On Exodus 4:22 — “And thou shalt say unto Pharaoh, Thus saith the LORD, Israel is my son, even my firstborn” — the witnesses mark Israel named God’s firstborn son.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: Pharaoh was to know the relation of Jehovah to Israel. Adam Clarke: the Hebrew people unutterably dear to God. Ellicott: this tender relation, now first revealed, a reality, not mere metaphor; the Israel of God enjoys the sonship of adoption in the True Son (cf. Hosea 11:1). Wesley: the first time “Thus saith the LORD” is used; Israel precious, honourable, dear.

The verse gives the message’s ground. God calls Israel his firstborn son. The nation’s dignity grounds the demand for release.

So the witnesses see God arm Moses with a message that asserts Israel’s unique dignity before Pharaoh. The witnesses note the solemn formula: “Thus saith the LORD” — Wesley observes that this is the first time in Scripture that this preface, afterwards so constant in the prophets, is put into a man’s mouth; Moses speaks with the authority of God’s ambassador. They mark the great declaration: “Israel is my son, even my firstborn” — God claims the nation as his own son, and his firstborn, the dearest and most honoured. They observe the expositors’ range of comment. Clarke renders the sense plainly: the Hebrew people are unutterably dear to God. Ellicott presses deeper, that this tender relation, now first revealed, is no mere metaphor but a reality — the people of God enjoying the sonship of adoption, the figure that flowers in the New Testament and in the prophet’s “out of Egypt have I called my son” (Hosea 11:1); the Israel of God is taken into the True Son and made one with him. They note Calvin’s point that this declaration refutes by anticipation Pharaoh’s pretext for refusal: though Jacob had once freely submitted to Egypt, Israel was God’s firstborn, with a prior and higher claim. They observe that the sonship grounds the demand of the next verse: a father may rightly require the release of his son. They note the contrast set up — God’s firstborn against Pharaoh’s. The verse thus states the ground: Israel God’s firstborn son — on which, in the next verse, God demands the son’s release on pain of Pharaoh’s own firstborn.
23:
On Exodus 4:23 — “Let my son go, that he may serve me: and if thou refuse… behold, I will slay thy son, even thy firstborn” — the witnesses mark the demand and the threat against Pharaoh’s firstborn.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: the threat was not uttered till just before the tenth plague (Exodus 11:5); the speech here much abbreviated. Adam Clarke: Israel could not serve God in Egypt, sacrifice being the chief part. The Reformed Calvin: this was the final clause of the legation, the last warning. Wesley: as men deal with God’s people, let them expect to be so dealt with.

The verse gives the demand. Release my son to serve me, or I will slay your firstborn. The claim of the firstborn meets the threat of the firstborn.

So the witnesses see God turn the dignity of Israel into a peremptory demand on Pharaoh, with a dread threat behind it. The witnesses note the demand: “Let my son go, that he may serve me” — God requires the release of his firstborn that Israel may worship him; Clarke observes that they could not so serve God in Egypt, the chief part of their worship being sacrifice of animals the Egyptians held sacred. They mark the threat that backs the demand: “if thou refuse… I will slay thy son, even thy firstborn” — if Pharaoh will not release God’s firstborn, his own firstborn shall be struck; the divine sonship of Israel is answered by the threatened death of Pharaoh’s heir, in just retribution. They observe the expositors’ note on the timing: this terrible threat was not delivered at the outset, but, as Calvin and Ellicott explain, was the final clause of the message, pressed only when all else had failed, just before the tenth plague (Exodus 11:5); the speech here states in brief the whole compass of the embassy from first demand to last warning. They note Wesley’s principle of just dealing: as Pharaoh deals with God’s people, so shall he be dealt with; he who would slay the sons of Israel (Exodus 1:22) shall lose his own. They observe the fitting correspondence of firstborn for firstborn. They note that the demand is grounded in God’s right over his son. The verse thus gives the demand and threat: release my firstborn, or lose yours — before the narrative turns, startlingly, to the danger at the inn.
24:
On Exodus 4:24 — “And it came to pass by the way in the inn, that the LORD met him, and sought to kill him” — the witnesses mark the dark and difficult encounter at the inn.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: no “inn” as we mean, but a recognized resting-place; the LXX has “the angel of the Lord.” The Puritan Henry: a very difficult passage, to be made improving. The Reformed Calvin: “the LORD met him” in a bad sense, an adverse, hostile encounter. Wesley: the sin was Moses’ neglect to circumcise his son, an effect of his being unequally yoked with a Midianite indulgent of her child.

The verse records the sudden peril. At the resting-place the LORD met Moses and sought to kill him. A grave sin is visited on the way.

So the witnesses approach one of the darkest and most difficult passages of the narrative: God’s threatening of Moses’ life at the lodging-place. The witnesses note the setting: “by the way in the inn” — not an inn as we understand it, but, Ellicott explains, a recognized resting-place or halting-spot on the journey, with little or no shelter. They mark the alarming statement: “the LORD met him, and sought to kill him” — Calvin observes that “met” is here used in a bad sense, of an adverse, hostile encounter, as though God’s hand were against Moses to interrupt his journey; the Septuagint softens it to “the angel of the Lord,” God acting through an angel of death or by some sudden, deadly sickness. They observe the expositors’ reading of the cause, drawn from the next verses: the sin in question was Moses’ neglect to circumcise his son, the sign of the covenant; Wesley suggests this neglect arose from Moses’ being unequally yoked with a Midianite wife who was over-indulgent of her child, Moses too yielding to her. They note the gravity the witnesses find in it: that the very man sent to deliver God’s covenant people had failed to keep the covenant in his own house; God would not employ as the lawgiver of Israel one who slighted the seal of the covenant. They observe Henry’s caution that, though the passage is obscure, it may be made improving: God is jealous for his ordinances even in his chosen servants. They note that the peril falls on Moses (or, some hold, on the uncircumcised son) until the neglect is remedied. The verse thus records the dark encounter: the LORD sought to kill him — which Zipporah, in the next verse, averts by circumcision.
25:
On Exodus 4:25 — “Then Zipporah took a sharp stone, and cut off the foreskin of her son, and cast it at his feet, and said, Surely a bloody husband art thou to me” — the witnesses mark Zipporah’s act of circumcision.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: stone knives, regarded as purer than metal, were commonly used for circumcision (Joshua 5:2). The Reformed Gill: perceiving the neglect was the cause of the displeasure, and Moses too ill to do it, she did it herself. Calvin: she improperly assumed the office, seizing a stone hastily under the impulse of fear.

The verse records the remedy. Zipporah circumcises her son with a stone, distressed at the bloody deed. The covenant sign is restored to avert the wrath.

So the witnesses see Zipporah, under the pressure of the deadly danger, perform the neglected circumcision herself. The witnesses note her quick understanding and act: perceiving, as Gill explains, that the neglect to circumcise their son was the cause of God’s displeasure, and Moses being too stricken or ill to do it, Zipporah “took a sharp stone, and cut off the foreskin of her son.” They mark the expositors’ note on the instrument: a stone knife, which Ellicott observes was anciently used for circumcision, being reckoned purer than metal (Joshua 5:2). They observe Calvin’s comment that the wife here improperly assumed an office not properly hers, seizing the stone hastily, driven by fear in the emergency. They note the obscure and much-debated words and gesture that follow: she “cast it at his feet” — the foreskin laid at the feet of Moses (or, some think, touched to his feet or the child’s) — “and said, Surely a bloody husband art thou to me.” They observe the distress and even reproach in her words: Zipporah, a Midianite, recoiling at the bloody rite forced upon her by the peril, calls Moses a “husband of blood,” one whose life she had as it were redeemed by the blood of her child’s circumcision, or whom she reproaches for the bloody necessity. They note that, whatever the precise sense of her cry, the act is plain: the covenant sign is restored, and the wrath thereby turned aside. They observe that the neglected duty is done, though by an unwilling hand. The verse thus records the remedy: Zipporah’s circumcision of her son — upon which, the next verse says, the danger departs.
26:
On Exodus 4:26 — “So he let him go: then she said, A bloody husband thou art, because of the circumcision” — the witnesses mark the danger removed.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: God let Moses go — allowed him to recover, accepting Zipporah’s act as sufficient, albeit tardy, reparation; then she explained her words. The Reformed Gill: the Lord, or the angel, the disease and trembling, departed from him. Wesley: the destroying angel withdrew; on this occasion Moses likely sent Zipporah and the children back to her father.

The verse records the deliverance. The peril past, Zipporah repeats her bitter word. The covenant kept, Moses is spared.

So the witnesses see the deadly danger lifted once the covenant duty is done. The witnesses note the release: “so he let him go” — the expositors take it that God (or the angel, or the deadly sickness) let Moses go, sparing his life; Ellicott reads it that God allowed Moses to recover, accepting Zipporah’s act, late though it was, as sufficient reparation for the neglect. They mark Gill’s sense that the Lord, or the angel sent against him, the disease and trembling that had seized him, now departed; the threat was withdrawn the moment the child was circumcised. They observe the repetition of Zipporah’s words: “A bloody husband thou art, because of the circumcision” — she says again, now in explanation, that Moses is to her a husband of blood on account of the bloody rite she had been driven to perform; the verse appends her own reason for the strange cry. They note Wesley’s suggestion that, on this occasion of friction, Moses likely sent Zipporah and the children back to her father Jethro for the time, going on to Egypt himself — for they are not mentioned again until Jethro brings them to him later. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: God is jealous for his covenant and its sign even in his chosen servant, but, the breach repaired, restores his favour and spares. They note that obedience averts the wrath that disobedience kindled. They observe that the episode, dark as it is, ends in deliverance. The verse thus records the danger removed: Moses let go, the circumcision done — and the narrative turns to Aaron’s coming.
27:
On Exodus 4:27 — “And the LORD said to Aaron, Go into the wilderness to meet Moses. And he went, and met him in the mount of God, and kissed him” — the witnesses mark the meeting of the brothers.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: by some secret but powerful movement on Aaron’s mind, or by an angel, he was directed; so exactly that they met at the appointed place. Ellicott: he met Moses on the mount of God, in the Sinaitic region, by divine guidance. The Reformed Calvin: Aaron, supposing his brother dead in exile, hears with joy that he lives and is favoured.

The verse records the reunion. Aaron, sent by God, meets Moses at the mount and kisses him. The brothers are joined for the work.

So the witnesses see God bring the two brothers together at the holy mount, fulfilling his earlier word. The witnesses note the divine summons to Aaron: “the LORD said to Aaron, Go into the wilderness to meet Moses” — God, who had told Moses that Aaron was coming to meet him (v. 14), now moves Aaron to set out; Clarke observes that this was done by some secret but powerful working upon Aaron’s mind, or by an angel’s ministry, and so exactly that the brothers met at the very place appointed. They mark the place of meeting: “in the mount of God” — Horeb, the Sinaitic region where Moses had seen the burning bush; Ellicott notes that without divine guidance Aaron would not have known to come there. They observe the warmth of the reunion: “and kissed him” — the affectionate greeting of brothers long parted, Aaron having supposed, as Calvin suggests, that his brother had perhaps died in his long exile, and now overjoyed to find him alive and chosen of God for a great work. They note the providence that orders the meeting so precisely: both brothers, separately directed, converge at the right place and time, that they may join in the mission. They observe the encouragement this must have given Moses, to be met by his brother at the outset. They note that God’s promise of Aaron’s glad reception (v. 14) is here made good. The verse thus records the reunion: Aaron meets and kisses Moses at the mount — the two now to act together, as the next verses show.
28:
On Exodus 4:28 — “And Moses told Aaron all the words of the LORD who had sent him, and all the signs which he had commanded him” — the witnesses mark Moses informing Aaron.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace it. Ellicott: the words and signs — the three miracles of Exodus 4:3–9. The Reformed Gill: Moses declared his mission and the particulars to be said to Israel and Pharaoh, that Aaron, as his spokesman, might know. Wesley: fellow-servants of God in the same work should use mutual freedom and rightly understand one another.

The verse records the briefing. Moses tells Aaron all God’s words and signs. The spokesman is fully informed for his task.

So the witnesses see Moses fully acquaint Aaron with the whole commission, that the two might act as one. The witnesses note the thoroughness: “Moses told Aaron all the words of the LORD… and all the signs which he had commanded him” — he held nothing back, but declared to his brother both the message he had received and the wonders he was to work; Ellicott identifies the signs as the three miracles given earlier (vv. 3–9). They mark the reason Gill gives: since Aaron was to be Moses’ mouth and spokesman, opening the commission to Israel and to Pharaoh, he must know fully what God had said and what was to be done; the speaker cannot deliver what he has not first been told. They observe Wesley’s lesson drawn from the scene: those who are fellow-servants of God in the same work should use a mutual freedom and openness, taking care rightly and fully to understand one another, that they may labour together in harmony. They note the order this establishes: Moses the recipient of revelation, faithfully passing it to Aaron, who will then voice it — the partnership defined earlier (vv. 15–16) now put into practice. They observe that the briefing is the necessary preparation for their joint appearance before the elders and the king. They note that Moses is faithful to communicate all. The verse thus records the briefing: Moses tells Aaron all the words and signs — equipping them, in the next verses, to gather the elders.
29:
On Exodus 4:29 — “And Moses and Aaron went and gathered together all the elders of the children of Israel” — the witnesses mark the gathering of the elders.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: the two brothers returned together to Egypt and at once set about their work. The Reformed Gill and Calvin: they gathered the elders, since the great multitude could not be assembled in one place; the elders represented the people.

The verse records the assembly. Moses and Aaron gather Israel’s elders. The mission begins with the representatives of the people.

So the witnesses see Moses and Aaron, arrived in Egypt, begin their work by assembling the elders of Israel. The witnesses note the joint action: “Moses and Aaron went and gathered together” — the two brothers, now united in the commission, set about it at once; Ellicott observes that, having returned together from the Sinaitic region, they immediately addressed themselves to their task. They mark whom they gathered: “all the elders of the children of Israel” — the heads and representatives of the people. They observe the practical reason the expositors give: as Calvin and Gill note, the whole multitude of Israel, grown to a great nation, could not be assembled in one place; and so the elders were called together as the representatives of the people, to receive the message on their behalf and convey it to the rest. They note that this was both the orderly and the prudent course: to begin with the recognized leaders, who could then carry the word to the families and tribes. They observe that the gathering of the elders fulfils the first part of God’s instruction, to bring the message to Israel before going to Pharaoh (Exodus 3:16). They note that the deliverance is announced first to the people it concerns. They observe that the brothers act faithfully and without delay. The verse thus records the assembly: the elders gathered — before whom Aaron, in the next verse, speaks and works the signs.

So the witnesses see Moses and Aaron, arrived in Egypt, begin their work by assembling the elders of Israel. They note the joint action: “Moses and Aaron went and gathered together” — the two brothers, now united in the commission, set about it at once; Ellicott observes that, having returned together from the Sinaitic region, they immediately addressed themselves to the task. They mark whom they gathered: “all the elders of the children of Israel” — the heads and representatives of the people. They observe the practical reason the expositors give: as Calvin and Gill note, the whole multitude of Israel, grown to a great nation, could not be assembled in one place, and so the elders were called as the representatives of the people, to receive the message on their behalf and convey it to the rest. They note that the gathering of the elders fulfils the first part of God’s instruction, to bring the message to Israel before going to Pharaoh (Exodus 3:16); the deliverance is announced first to the people it concerns.
30:
On Exodus 4:30 — “And Aaron spake all the words which the LORD had spoken unto Moses, and did the signs in the sight of the people” — the witnesses mark Aaron’s speaking and the signs done.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: Aaron, likely better acquainted with the Hebrew tongue, became the spokesman; he did the signs — turned the rod to a serpent, made the hand leprous, and the like. Ellicott: Aaron spake according to the divine command (Exodus 4:16) and did the signs. The Reformed Gill: Aaron, as Moses’ mouth, declared what Moses had related to him.

The verse records the message delivered. Aaron speaks God’s words and the signs are done before the people. The appointed order is carried out.

So the witnesses see the partnership work just as God had ordained: Aaron speaks, and the signs are performed before the assembled people. The witnesses note Aaron’s office fulfilled: “Aaron spake all the words which the LORD had spoken unto Moses” — as the appointed spokesman, Aaron declares to the people the whole message Moses had received and conveyed to him; Gill marks this as Aaron acting as Moses’ mouth, and Ellicott as the carrying out of the divine command (v. 16). They observe Clarke’s suggestion that Aaron, having dwelt in Egypt, was likely the more fluent in the Hebrew tongue and so the fitter to address the people. They mark the signs done: “and did the signs in the sight of the people” — the three wonders given to Moses (the rod to serpent, the leprous and healed hand, and the like) were now performed before the eyes of the elders, to confirm the message; the expositors note that, though Aaron is named as the speaker and (with Moses) the doer, the signs are the same God had committed to Moses. They observe that the whole arrangement defined at the mount is here put into action: God’s words, through Moses to Aaron to the people, and the confirming signs. They note that the message is both spoken and visibly authenticated. They observe that the mission proceeds in good order. The verse thus records the delivery: Aaron speaks and the signs are done — with the people’s response in the next verse.
31:
On Exodus 4:31 — “And the people believed: and when they heard that the LORD had visited the children of Israel, and that he had looked upon their affliction, then they bowed their heads and worshipped” — the witnesses mark the people’s belief and worship.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: they credited the divine appointment of Moses and Aaron, the miracles confirming the testimony; they bowed their heads and worshipped. The Reformed Gill: they believed Moses was sent of God and would be their deliverer. Calvin: a part put for the whole, or all with one consent embraced the message; their solemn adoration.

The verse records the response. The people believe, and hearing of God’s visitation, bow and worship. Faith answers the word and the signs.

So the witnesses see the people of Israel receive the message with faith and adoration — in contrast to Moses’ fears. The witnesses note the belief: “the people believed” — the very thing Moses had feared would not happen (v. 1) comes to pass; Israel credits the divine appointment of Moses and Aaron, the signs confirming the spoken testimony, as Clarke observes; Gill notes they believed Moses was sent of God and would be their deliverer. They mark the substance of what they heard and believed: “that the LORD had visited the children of Israel, and that he had looked upon their affliction” — that God had at last regarded his people in their bondage, remembering his covenant and coming to their relief; the long-awaited visitation had begun. They observe the response of worship: “then they bowed their heads and worshipped” — overcome with the good news of deliverance, the people bow in reverent, thankful adoration of God. They note Calvin’s observation that this may be a part put for the whole, or that all with one consent embraced the message; the solemn worship shows the depth of their belief and gratitude. They observe the encouragement this gave to Moses, whose every objection of unbelief on the people’s part is here answered: they believe and worship at once. They note that the chapter, opened with Moses’ fear of unbelief, closes with the people’s faith and adoration. They observe that God’s word, attended by his signs, prevails. The verse thus records the response: the people believe and worship — the hopeful close of the call of Moses.
